pageTitle.value: Margaret Atwood and Naomi Alderman - Video

pageCaption.value: Canada’s grand mistress of fiction Margaret Atwood likens mentoring to door-opening. “We speak the same language,” Atwood says of her protégée, young British author Naomi Alderman. Their relationship even extends to writing a zombie novella together.

description.value: Rolex Mentor and Protégée in Literature, 2012 - 2013

pageTitle.value: Wole Soyinka and Tara June Winch - Video

pageCaption.value: Declaring their mentorship to be “a process of development”, Wole Soyinka, first African Laureate of the Nobel Prize for Literature, encourages his protégée, Tara June Winch, as she agonizes over her writing. When anyone writes, he says, himself included, “it’s all over the place.” It’s the rewriting that follows that marks out a true writer.

WaPageTitle.value: Wole Soyinka and Tara June Winch - Video

pageTitle.value: Mia Couto and Julián Fuks - Video

pageCaption.value: Mia Couto’s mentoring relationship with Julián Fuks was a fusion of interests wrought by their mutual experiences as writers living in former colonies of Portugal. Liberated from his “autofiction” style of writing by his mentor, Fuks found he was able to explore his imagination in a different way.

WaPageTitle.value: Mia Couto and Julián Fuks - Video

pageTitle.value: Hans Magnus Enzensberger and Tracy K. Smith - Video

pageCaption.value: In a mentorship that they describe as an intense and joyful conversation, poets Hans Magnus Enzensberger (from Germany) and Tracy K. Smith (American) explore the nature of poetry – “Everyone has poetry in their head,” Enzensberger proclaims. But their quest goes further, examining history and identity as Smith realizes a longstanding desire to write her family memoir.

WaPageTitle.value: Hans Magnus Enzensberger and Tracy K. Smith - Video

pageTitle.value: Gilberto Gil and Dina Elwedidi - Video

pageCaption.value: For Egyptian singer Dina Elwedidi and her mentor, Brazilian music icon Gilberto Gil, differences in culture are not an obstacle to a productive mentorship. Quite the opposite. “She wanted to experience that friction between Egypt and Brazil,” Gil says.

WaPageTitle.value: Gilberto Gil and Dina Elwedidi - Video

pageTitle.value: Patrice Chéreau and Michał Borczuch - Video

pageCaption.value: The late French director Patrice Chéreau described himself as “a slave to the text”. His protégé Michał Borczuch takes the opposite approach, with an “anarchistic” style of direction. “I wasn’t interested in somebody who was doing something similar to me,” Chéreau said.

WaPageTitle.value: Patrice Chéreau and Michał Borczuch - Video

pageTitle.value: William Kentridge and Mateo López - Video

pageCaption.value: Visual arts protégé Mateo López is, thanks to his architecture background, “incredibly precise”, says mentor William Kentridge. In his Johannesburg studio, Kentridge pushes his protégé out of his “comfort zone”, helping him to develop a more spontaneous approach to creativity.

WaPageTitle.value: William Kentridge and Mateo López - Video
